moment_text: Oh, wow. So I. Found my lump doing a, uh, self breath exam at home in the shower. And then, uh, I felt the lump and I was like, no, this is not, you know, a lump. It just felt different before then. I had had cysts in that particular breath and I've had them removed, but it just felt different, um, than the cysts that I had felt before. It was bigger. Um, and so I was like, okay, I'm gonna go to work. I worked with, I had a coworker that I worked with. We were very close, and so, you know, I said to her, I said, listen, I'm not trying to be funny, but I really want you to feel this. And she was like, what? And I was like, no, I really need you to feel this. I need to know that I'm not going crazy. And so she, um, said, okay. We, uh, went into our little office that we had like, that connected our rooms 'cause we were teaching teachers. And, um, she felt the lump and she looked at me and she said, Ika, you need to go see your doctor. And that, that was something I already knew.
eligibility_rationale: This moment passes the evidence gate (A2) because it contains agency signals (seeking support) and emotional signals (self-doubt) within a peer community system interaction.

moment_text: Um, honestly, I had felt like it was cancer way before I found out it was. I don't think I've ever said that in any interview though, but I did feel, feel it. Um, I went, uh, that, that began my journey to finding out really what it was that I was feeling in my breast. So I contacted my, um, gynecologist. I let her know, um, what I felt. She, um, sent me right away for a mammogram. No, she had me come in for a sonogram. Um, they looked at it through a sonogram and then she sent me for a mammogram once she sent me for the mammogram. Um, and the machine, you know, the machine goes, closes on your breath and squeezes it. I had discharge come outta my nipple and. As soon as the discharge came outta my nipple, I knew right away that it was breast cancer.
eligibility_rationale: This moment passes the evidence gate (A2) because it contains agency signals (seeking care) and emotional signals (fear, clarity) within a healthcare provider system interaction.

moment_text: Um, to be honest with you, the, uh, radiologist asked for me to stay so that, that they could speak with me. The radiologist showed me what the lump was, and she did say that it does look like breast cancer. She said, I'm not a doctor, but it does look like breast cancer. So, I mean, I, that appointment, um, to me that came from her was official. It was official at that moment. Um, but it took about, um, two weeks before I saw, um, my doctor and they told me that it was, uh, it was, uh, breast cancer.
eligibility_rationale: This moment passes the evidence gate (A2) because it contains a barrier signal (delayed diagnosis) and agency signals (enduring) within a healthcare provider system interaction.
notes_for_reviewer: The two-week wait for the official confirmation after being told it looked like cancer is tagged as a delayed diagnosis.

moment_text: I was in a relationship or what I thought was a relationship, to be honest with you, um, with someone that I had been with for wow, like probably, uh, three years at that point. I think. Um, and yeah. He was overseas, he was coming home, we were making plans to be together. And he went with me to my appointment. Um, he said, I'm gonna be there for you. Um, and, uh, it didn't work out that way. He decided that he wanted to be somewhere else, and that's where he went. Um, I felt like the plans for my life or the plans that I had, um, were shattered at that moment. Uh, when I, when I got diagnosed, like I just, all these plans that I had went away. I felt like cancer took so much from me. I still kind of feel that way a little bit, you know, because I am, um. I'm still fighting Uhhuh, so I just, sometimes I just feel like cancer just continues to take things from me, but I continue to fight.
eligibility_rationale: This moment passes the evidence gate (A2) because it contains barrier signals (lack of support) and strong emotional signals (grief, hopelessness) within a family system interaction.

moment_text: Um, I think I. I trusted the doctors that I had, to be honest with you. Um, I trusted what they were saying and the plans that they had for me. Um, and so I just let them, you know, kind of take control. This was actually my second battle with canceling. I was diagnosed with cancer as a child. Um, I had osteosarcoma, which is cancer of the femur. So, um, I kind of knew what the, what the fight was gonna be like because I had had chemo before, so I just wanted to get it over with. I want it to be done with, so whatever it is that they told me I needed to do, I did it. Except for the decision when it came to my breast, um, the surgeon felt like I should only have a lumpectomy, but I didn't want a lumpectomy. I just said, you know, just take 'em both. I felt like, like I didn't want it to come back. I didn't, you know, I was like, if I, if I get 'em both taken off, maybe it won't come back. And that was a myth, but, you know. Um, yeah, I just felt like, okay, they won't come back if I get 'em both taken off. And I just wanted to do what I felt was best. And, and at the time that was what was, was having a, a bilateral mastectomy, um, doing the chemo that the doctor rec recommended.
eligibility_rationale: This moment passes the evidence gate (A2) because it contains strong agency signals (self-advocacy, making decisions) and emotional signals (fear, trust) within a healthcare provider system interaction.
notes_for_reviewer: This moment shows a contrast between trusting doctors for chemo but advocating for her own choice regarding surgery.

moment_text: although I, I came to find out. That there was some things that were missing that I didn't have that other people who had the same type of cancer, the breast cancer as I did, had. And so it was like, you know, if I would've had this drug, would I, would, you know, would have, would the cancer have returned? Um, I don't know, you know, but that's, that's what happened.
eligibility_rationale: This moment passes the evidence gate (A2) because it contains barrier signals (lack of information, fragmented care) and emotional signals (self-doubt) within a healthcare provider system interaction.

moment_text: I didn't meet anybody until, um, after my journey was over. I didn't meet anybody my age until after my initial journey was over. And what led you to that? Did you seek it out or? Um, actually my cousin, she had, had, um, had known someone who knew someone who had an organization and I got linked up with that organization through an event that I went to, um, with my cousin.
eligibility_rationale: This moment passes the evidence gate (A2) because it contains barrier signals (social isolation) and emotional signals (isolation) within a peer community system interaction.

moment_text: I think the most manageable aspect was the mastectomy part. Um. That seemed like it was pretty easy having the surgery. Uh, my, uh, surgeon, my breast surgeon, she was amazing. My plastic surgeon was amazing. Um, she, uh, very supportive. She knew what she was doing. She was very knowledgeable. Um, one of the best, I read up on her, I looked like, you know, when it came to the plastic surgery part, it was like, hold on. I need to find somebody who's the best or who is close to it because baby, I don't want these things looking crazy. And so that's what I did. I found somebody who I, who was ranked very high, and I, I, I'm very satisfied with how my breast came out.
eligibility_rationale: This moment passes the evidence gate (A2) because it contains agency signals (seeking information) and emotional signals (confidence) within a healthcare provider system interaction.
notes_for_reviewer: This is a positive experience moment, highlighting agency and trust.

moment_text: Um, I was a single mom, so I think that, you know, the drugs, the side effects of the drugs. And then being a mom, my daughter was six at the time. So just trying to be there for her and with her while also fighting was, was very hard because the side effects of the drug, they make you tired, you're sick, you know? And you have a 6-year-old like who wants to run around and do things and jump on you and you know, like, come on mommy. You know that and, and who was in sports? Um, you know, doing activities and you didn't want her life to be ruined by what you were going through or, or uplifted, you know, uprooted by what you were going through. So you were trying to keep things as normal as possible, you know, that's what I was trying to do, was keep things as normal as possible for her. Um, she was my main, uh, thought through everything that I was going through.
eligibility_rationale: This moment passes the evidence gate (A2) because it contains barrier signals (lack of support) and emotional signals (overwhelm) within a family system interaction.
notes_for_reviewer: The barrier 'lack of support' is inferred from her status as a single mom managing care and parenting alone.

moment_text: so because my cancer metastasized when I was 36, um, my life definitely doesn't look like what I had thought. Um, what I had dreamed, what I had hoped. Um, so I continue to fight. Um, plans, treatment plans change. Um, I'm in what they call the leap, the leapfrog phase, you know, jumping from lily pad to lily pad right now because, um, a drug may work for a certain amount of time and then it'll stop, or a drug may not work. I may start it and it may not be for me. So then I jumped to the next one and I'm jumping to the next one, and I'm jumping to the next one at least until there's no more, you know, drugs for me to take at this point. You know, things are dwindling down for me, um, where there aren't a lot, a lot of options at this point. So, um, that's where I am currently. In my, on my path. On, on, on the journey. Mm-hmm.
eligibility_rationale: This moment is eligible as it details a lived experience with metastatic cancer treatment, containing clear emotional signals (sadness, hopelessness) and a barrier signal (fragmented care) within a healthcare provider interaction.

moment_text: Absolutely. I often put on a brave face. And people don't see what goes on behind the scene. You know, I am talking to my doctors about progression. Um, I'm talking to my doctors about pain that I'm having or you know, just something new that's going on that I'm feeling. I am weekly in therapy. Trying to maintain mentally, like just trying to be strong for myself, for my daughter, for my family. Um, just trying to, you know, keep a a, a clear mind and a clear heart.
eligibility_rationale: This moment is eligible because it has strong agency signals (speaking_up, seeking_care) and emotional signals (overwhelm) within a healthcare provider system interaction, meeting the evidence gate requirements.

moment_text: I do feel like I have, I've had enough support. I have, um, a large family. I have my team, my oncology team. I also have palliative care team, my palliative care teams. That's where I am currently. Um, in this space, uh, I do feel like I have a good amount of support. I'm grateful for it. Because without them I don't, I don't know where, where I would be, because there's some days where I'm just like, I don't know what to do. I don't know which way to turn. And I'm calling them like, I need help today, mom. Mentally I'm just not there, you know? And it's either I'm talking to the right person or they get me to the right person. So. I don't think so. I think I'm one of the lucky one. Honestly. I think that I'm rare. I, what I get is rare, and it's unfortunate because we do need more support as young people.
eligibility_rationale: This moment is eligible because it identifies a systemic barrier (lack_of_support for young people) and contains clear agency signals (seeking_support) and emotional signals (overwhelm, relief), making it a strong candidate for pattern analysis.

moment_text: There are some, you know, groups out there. That, uh, support groups out there that we can link up with. Now, especially as young and black women, there are more support groups, uh, coming out and, and being known. Um, unfortunately because we are those types of groups, there are places that aren't, you know, with everything that's going on now, they're not being funded. The way that other organizations are being funded. If you get my drill,
eligibility_rationale: This moment is eligible as it explicitly identifies a systemic barrier (cost_constraint/funding disparity) impacting a specific community, which passes the evidence gate.
notes_for_reviewer: The speaker is alluding to racial bias in funding for support organizations.

moment_text: I always say that, uh, quality of life today would be just being able to live life the way that you wanna live according to how you see it. That would, would be how I would describe quality of life today, not being held back by the side effects of the drugs, the, the drugs itself, you know, the process of this journey, but just being able to live life. The way you see it. The way you want to.
eligibility_rationale: This moment is eligible because it contains emotional signals (hope, frustration) and agency signals (making_decisions) related to the patient's experience, passing the evidence gate.

moment_text: treatment, my treatment plan, and my care. With my, uh, oncology team, that's the most manageable part because I have so much support with my palliative team taking over the pain, uh, part of it. And so, you know, my, my oncology team just focuses on, you know, the treatment, the treatment portion of it. So I think that that's the easy part.
eligibility_rationale: This moment is eligible as it describes a positive interaction with the healthcare system, supported by emotional signals (relief, trust), which can be used to identify protective factors or successful care models.
notes_for_reviewer: This moment highlights a protective factor: integrated palliative and oncology care.

moment_text: The most overwhelming aspect to manage has been my, um, healthcare. Um, I am on the line of making too much money and making not enough. So I have to pay for healthcare and I don't work because of the pain, because of the treatment, because of, I don't have the energy, um, because of the neuropathy, because I don't sleep at night because I can't work. But I have to pay for healthcare, which costs me. A good $14,000 a year. I have $2,000 copay for medication and I don't work. So, um, that is the hardest part to manage is getting people to understand like, where's this money gonna come from? What am I going to do? You know, I, I just, I don't know. There sometimes I'm just like, don't, don't know. Can't, can't help you. Yeah. You know, if you see my bills, you'll be like, what? You know, I'm in and out of the hospital. I, I just spent 14, 4, 4 weeks in the hospital.
eligibility_rationale: This moment is highly eligible as it details a significant barrier_encounter (cost_constraint) with strong emotional signals (overwhelm, hopelessness, frustration) within the healthcare system.

moment_text: Yeah. I truly don't think that lawmakers get it, you know? I don't think people get it like. I'm out here trying to fight for my life, and then I have to fight you guys too just to be able to take care of myself.
eligibility_rationale: This moment is eligible due to its strong expression of a systemic barrier (cost_constraint), coupled with clear emotional signals (frustration, anger) and an act of agency (speaking_up).

moment_text: Um, peer support. I work with Tiger Lily, so I, I'm a angel advocate with Tiger Lily. So the peer support with them is amazing. Um, they're always there. Um, so I'm, I'm grateful to have MEMA and the crew, everybody that you know on her team. They are always rallying behind me and sending me lots of love and positive energy. So that's very, very helpful. Um, and you know, I, I've met a lot of people through the organization, um, at, at different events. I've met a lot of people so that I have like, you know, peer support, just individual peer support. My, I don't have a nurse navigator, but I do have like social workers at the hospital that support me, um, and help me with finding money or, you know, help me with, they were helping me with finding scholarships for my daughter and, uh, fi help me finding grant money to help pay bills and things like that. So, um, I have, I have nurses and I have, um. Social workers to help with those things. So I'm grateful for the amount of support that I do have within, um, the cancer community.
eligibility_rationale: This moment is eligible because it details a protective factor (peer and institutional support) and includes clear emotional signals (relief, connection) and agency signals (seeking_support), making it valuable for understanding positive system interactions.

moment_text: you know, healthcare as a whole, um, for not just younger people, but you know, as, as a young black woman I've experienced so much, especially going into the er. And I feel like this is what needs to be improved. Like us being able to go in the ER and say that we're in pain and not be looked at as a drug addict, um, someone seeking, you know, fix. Because I've been looked at as that. I, I've been told, you know, we don't have any drug, any, anything higher than what you have at home. You know, um, not being heard or listened to when you're talking about your pain not being believed. Um, it, it, you know, the healthcare system really needs to be improved when it comes to bedside manner, when it comes to health equity. Um, yeah, so I would say the healthcare system and their, the health equity.
eligibility_rationale: This moment is highly eligible as it is a direct account of a significant barrier_encounter (provider_dismissal, lack_of_validation) rooted in systemic issues, and it is rich with emotional and agency signals.

moment_text: Around hope. Um, I feel like people think that we might not have hope, you know, when we're, when we enter into stage four, or if we have stage four and, and we're, I, I think that people feel like we, we lose our hope, but we don't, you know, sometimes it just gets a little hard, especially when you've been fighting.
eligibility_rationale: This moment is eligible because it identifies a barrier (stigma/misconception), contains clear emotional signals (hope, frustration), and demonstrates agency (speaking_up) in a community context.

moment_text: Long, you, you, you've made it beyond the five years, right? And you've been fighting and you keep fighting. It's hard. Um, and we don't give up, but we do get tired. So I, I want people to understand that we do have hope, but we do get tired. Um, as far as quality of life, I think that. For me, quality of life comes first. At this point. I mean, I know that miracles can happen. I wish. I hope that one day I can wake up and be that miracle, right? But until then, my quality of life comes first. Like I'm gonna. Make sure that I'm not, I don't have to live every day in pain or I don't have to live every day, uh, with those side effects of these drugs taking, you know, living at the forefront of my life. I wanna be able to live and, and make special memories, and have special moments with my family and friends and my, especially my daughter while I'm still here. And sometimes we can't do that on certain drugs. So I'm not willing to take a drug that's going to not allow me to have a good quality of life.
eligibility_rationale: This moment is eligible because it contains strong agency signals (making_decisions, setting_boundaries) and a barrier signal (time_constraint) within a healthcare_provider interaction.
notes_for_reviewer: The speaker's decision to prioritize quality of life over treatments with severe side effects is a powerful example of patient agency.

moment_text: Um, I think that advocacy is important because we are our own best advocate. Like, nobody's gonna root for you or stand up for you better than you, right? Or, uh, we, we have to educate ourselves and know. You know, what's happening with ourselves, what's happening in our lives in order to be the best advocate we can be for ourselves. So, you know, educating ourselves. I think that, you know, younger people are changing what advocacy looks like because we're educating ourselves and we are, you know, learning more about it and what, and know what it looks like, right. And what it should look like. And we're pushing against those walls or those boundaries of pushing them more and more each day. Right? And, and people are like, wow. Like, okay, maybe, maybe this is what we're supposed to be doing. Like maybe this is the right way to go. Right? So I think that, yeah, you know, we're getting younger and younger and we're understanding more and we're educating ourselves more about. Cancer about, um, quality of life, about policies, about everything. Cancer, right? Cancer related. And so we're like, okay, we're gonna push the boundaries and we do it. We don't just talk about it, we actually do it.
eligibility_rationale: This moment is eligible because it contains strong agency signals (self_advocacy, seeking_information) and emotional signals within the context of a system interaction.
notes_for_reviewer: The speaker connects self-education directly to the power of advocacy, particularly for younger generations.

moment_text: Mm-hmm. Yeah. I, I mean, I think. The younger generation, like there's people complain about, you know, instant gratification, like, oh, you don't know how to wait. But I think that that's powerful, um, when it comes to pushing things forward because just saying we don't have the answer or we've never done it that way isn't the full answer. That's just like the starting point, right?
eligibility_rationale: This moment is eligible because it identifies a barrier signal (provider_dismissal) and an agency signal (speaking_up) within a healthcare system interaction.
notes_for_reviewer: The host reframes a generational stereotype ('instant gratification') as a powerful tool for challenging systemic inertia ('we've never done it that way').

moment_text: never give up on yourself. The second thing is, um. I feel like, and I've always said this, like this journey is a battle of the mind. It's, it's more of a battle of the mind than the body. And once your mind goes, your body goes, it follows, right. I feel like that, that's, that's always what I've said. I've just watched people around me, right. And I've watched myself. But, um, so just keep your mind strong. Keep your, keep your mind strong, speak life into yourself and into your situation. Um, and the last thing I would say would be, be strong and courageous. This journey. Journey. Like I wish I can really say that differently though, but trying to think of how I can say that differently. Just be strong and courageous.
eligibility_rationale: This moment is eligible because it contains emotional and agency signals directed at a peer_community, meeting the evidence gate requirements.
notes_for_reviewer: This is direct advice to other patients, emphasizing mental fortitude as a primary tool in the cancer journey.

moment_text: Um, that's come from me. Like that's just come from my experience. This is like, you know, my third battle with, with cancer. And so along the way I picked up some gem. One of those gems is like knowing that this is a battle of the mind and mental health is important on this journey, you know? So I just feel like in order to fight it, you have to be strong mentally. It's not, you know, your body has to be strong, but in order to keep your body strong, you have to know mentally what to do. What to eat, you know, what, how to pray, how to meditate, you know? Uh, there's so many different things. So I've learned like it's not just Western medicine that keeps you healthy. So I'm, I, I do the other things to keep me healthy too. Some holistic things I added into my, my journey.
eligibility_rationale: This moment is eligible because it contains agency signals (making_decisions) and a barrier signal (education_gap) in the context of a healthcare_provider interaction.
notes_for_reviewer: The speaker explicitly contrasts her learned, holistic approach with the limitations of 'just Western medicine,' highlighting a perceived education gap in conventional care.
